Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty inconsistent even though I put in a lot of hours. I'd often have these intense bursts where I'd study for hours straight, then go days without touching anything. I preferred reading through materials and taking detailed written notes rather than following any structured schedule. With six online courses, I kind of just bounced between whatever felt most urgent at the moment.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines and my mood. I'd tell myself I'd study at the same time each day, but that rarely happened. When assignments were due, I'd cram everything into a few days. Otherwise, I'd drift and do other things with my extracurriculars or just procrastinate because the motivation wasn't always there despite knowing I should be working.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline approaching or getting a notification about something. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if anything new was posted, but I wouldn't necessarily engage deeply. If a peer mentioned something in our group chat about course content, that would prompt me to check the LMS too. It wasn't really a habit, more reactive than proactive.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became a natural part of my routine. I'd go through phases where I'd check it more regularly, especially after connecting with some classmates through the peer support program. But those habits never stuck for long. It always felt like something I had to force myself to do rather than something automatic.
Q5: The reading materials and lecture transcripts were most useful for me since I learn best through reading and writing. I'd download PDFs and annotate them extensively. The discussion forums were occasionally helpful when peers explained concepts differently than the instructor. I appreciated having everything organized in one place even if I didn't engage with it all online.
Q6: I barely watched the videos, honestly. I'd skim through them or just read the transcripts instead because watching felt like it took forever. Some of the optional practice quizzes I ignored completely because they weren't graded. The discussion posts I'd often read but not contribute to unless I absolutely had to. It just felt like extra work that wouldn't impact my grade directly.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to exam preparation or assignments. I needed to see the immediate relevance. If something helped me understand a concept I was struggling with or if it would clearly improve my grade, I'd engage. Abstract "enrichment" materials felt like a luxury I couldn't justify when I had so much else going on.
Q8: Optional activities felt completely different. Required ones had this weight to them, this urgency that made me actually do them even when I didn't want to. Optional stuff? I'd tell myself I'd get to it later and never did. There's something about knowing something counts toward your grade that changes how you prioritize it. Optional felt like a suggestion I could ignore.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my actual studying happened offline. I'd download all the readings at the start of the week and work through them on my tablet without internet. I have notebooks full of handwritten summaries and concept maps. I also used external resources like textbooks from the library and YouTube explanations for topics I found confusing. The LMS was just the starting point.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours reviewing my own notes or rewriting summaries to prepare for exams, none of which showed up anywhere. I'd have study sessions with peers from the peer support group where we'd quiz each other using printed materials. I'd also do practice problems from textbooks that weren't tracked. My best learning happened away from the screen.
Q11: All the time. I'd access a PDF once, download it, and never open it on the LMS again. Same with lecture slides. I preferred having everything saved locally so I could study without needing internet or dealing with the LMS interface. My downloaded folder was basically my real course library. The single access probably made it look like I barely engaged with materials.
Q12: Around midterms, everything felt overwhelming even though my stress levels were already pretty high baseline. I was juggling all six courses and my extracurriculars, and some weeks I just couldn't bring myself to care about certain modules. The peer support helped pull me back, but there were definitely weeks where I was just going through the motions without really absorbing anything.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or anxious about other courses, I'd do the bare minimum on the LMS. I'd open things without really reading them, just to feel like I was making progress. Competing responsibilities from my other activities meant the LMS often got deprioritized. I'd batch everything into late-night sessions which wasn't ideal for actual learning.
Q14: Yes, more often than I'd like to admit. I'd have the LMS open, scrolling through materials, but my mind was elsewhere. Sometimes I'd read the same paragraph three times without processing it. It looked like engagement from the outside, but internally I was checked out. Those sessions felt productive in the moment but I'd retain almost nothing.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly even knowing they were important. The 57% completion rate reflects that. I think it was a mix of anxiety about not doing them perfectly and just feeling disconnected from the course sometimes. I'd avoid discussion posts especially because putting my thoughts out there felt vulnerable. I always meant to catch up but often didn't.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually submit work. I'd have this burst of activity in the 24-48 hours before something was due. My LMS logins probably spiked dramatically around due dates. Without deadlines, I'd drift indefinitely. It's not a healthy pattern but it's how I functioned. The pressure was necessary motivation.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. Good feedback motivated me to engage more with similar activities. Critical feedback sometimes made me avoid that type of work for a while because of my anxiety. When instructors gave specific, constructive comments, I'd actually go back and review related materials. Generic feedback felt pointless and didn't change my behavior at all.
Q18: The course structure gave me flexibility which was both good and bad. Good because I could work around my other commitments, bad because I'd abuse that flexibility and procrastinate. Having clear modules helped me know what to prioritize. But when things were too open-ended, I'd flounder. I needed more guardrails than the structure provided.
Q19: Having readings available as downloadable PDFs made engagement easier for my learning style. The discussion requirements felt forced and made engagement harder because I'm not naturally inclined to post publicly. Weekly deadlines helped create some rhythm, but the sheer volume across six courses made it hard to give any single course deep attention. Simpler navigation would have helped too.
Q20: I think that could be students like me who are anxious about submitting imperfect work. You might explore everything, read all the materials, watch videos, but then freeze when it comes to actually producing something. Or they could be confused about requirements and keep revisiting things without making progress. High activity doesn't mean high confidence or clarity.
Q21: That's basically me. I performed well on exams because I studied extensively offline and I'm good at test-taking. My low LMS activity doesn't reflect the hours I spent with downloaded materials and my own notes. Some of us just don't need to be constantly logged in to learn effectively. The platform captures presence, not preparation.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active and what you click on. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if I'm deeply processing material or mindlessly scrolling. They miss all offline studying, peer discussions, and the mental work of synthesizing information. Logs capture behavior, not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when you're curious, asking questions, making connections between concepts. For me, engagement feels like being mentally present and invested in my own learning. It's not about how many times I log in but whether I'm genuinely trying to grow from the experience.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show the hours I spent in study groups with peers from the support program, talking through concepts until they clicked. It wouldn't show my anxiety before submitting work or the relief after exams. It can't capture that I was genuinely happy when I finally understood difficult topics, or how peer support improved my overall mood and kept me from completely disengaging. The human elements are invisible.
